agent_output:
{
  "summary": {
    "verdict": "BLOCK",
    "one_liner": "Memory leak from never-cleaned-up CourierTracker instances and WebSocket listeners is the root cause of steadily increasing memory usage"
  },
  "critical_findings": [
    {
      "title": "Memory leak: CourierTracker instances never cleaned up on disconnect",
      "risk": "Each courier reconnect (avg every 5 min) creates a new CourierTracker with bound event listeners on the WebSocket, but there is no 'close' handler to remove listeners or release the instance. Over 3 months, thousands of orphaned CourierTracker objects and their closures accumulate in memory, explaining the observed OOM trajectory.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "CourierTracker.setupListeners()",
      "reproduction": "1. Connect courier via WebSocket — new CourierTracker created. 2. Courier disconnects (WebSocket closes). 3. No 'close' event handler fires, so the CourierTracker reference persists. 4. Courier reconnects — another CourierTracker is created. 5. Repeat every ~5 minutes per courier. With 100 couriers over 3 months: ~2.6M leaked instances.",
      "fix": "Add a 'close' listener that removes all WebSocket event listeners and nullifies references: `this.ws.on('close', () => { this.ws.removeAllListeners(); this.ws = null; });` Also ensure the caller (connection manager) drops its reference to the CourierTracker on close.",
      "time_to_fix": "30 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Service stability / memory usage must remain bounded over time"
    },
    {
      "title": "Unguarded JSON.parse crashes the process on malformed WebSocket messages",
      "risk": "A single malformed message from any courier will throw an uncaught exception inside the WebSocket 'message' handler. In Node.js, this crashes the process (or at minimum kills the connection handler), taking down tracking for all couriers if the process exits.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "CourierTracker.setupListeners() — `JSON.parse(data)` in message handler",
      "reproduction": "Send a non-JSON string (e.g. empty string, binary ping frame, truncated payload) over the WebSocket. JSON.parse throws SyntaxError. No try/catch exists. Process crashes or the event loop rejects the promise.",
      "fix": "Wrap in try/catch: `try { const location = JSON.parse(data); this.updateLocation(location); } catch (e) { console.error('Invalid message from courier', this.courierId, e); }`",
      "time_to_fix": "10 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Service availability — single bad message must not crash the service"
    },
    {
      "title": "No validation on location payload enables database corruption",
      "risk": "The location object is passed directly to the DB update with no validation. A message like `{\"lat\": \"hello\", \"lng\": null}` or `{}` writes garbage (undefined/NaN) into the courier location table. Downstream systems (ETA calculation, customer map view) will silently consume corrupted coordinates.",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "CourierTracker.updateLocation()",
      "reproduction": "Send `{\"lat\": 999, \"lng\": -999}` or `{\"foo\": 1}` via WebSocket. updateLocation calls db with `undefined` or out-of-range values. No error thrown — silent data corruption.",
      "fix": "Validate before writing: `if (typeof location.lat !== 'number' || typeof location.lng !== 'number' || Math.abs(location.lat) > 90 || Math.abs(location.lng) > 180) return;`",
      "time_to_fix": "15 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Data integrity — courier locations must be valid GPS coordinates"
    }
  ],
  "important_issues": [
    {
      "title": "Database write has no error handling",
      "description": "db.couriers.updateLocation() is called without await or .catch(). If the DB is slow or down, errors are silently swallowed (or become unhandled promise rejections in Node 18+, crashing the process).",
      "location": "CourierTracker.updateLocation()"
    },
    {
      "title": "No rate limiting on location updates",
      "description": "A misbehaving or compromised courier client can flood the WebSocket with rapid location updates, hammering the database with writes. There is no throttle/debounce to cap update frequency (e.g. 1 update/second).",
      "location": "CourierTracker.setupListeners() — message handler"
    }
  ],
  "suggestions": [
    "Add a debounce/throttle (e.g. 1 update per second) to updateLocation to protect the database under high-frequency GPS reporting",
    "Use structured logging instead of console.error for production observability",
    "Consider batching location updates to reduce per-message DB write overhead"
  ],
  "production_readiness": {
    "deployment_risks": "The memory leak is already in production and worsening. Fix must be deployed promptly. No migration or schema change needed — this is a code-only fix.",
    "rollback_capability": "Safe to roll back — the fix only adds cleanup behavior. Rolling back returns to the current (leaking) state, which is the baseline.",
    "backward_compatibility": "No breaking changes. WebSocket protocol and DB schema are unaffected.",
    "monitoring_coverage": "Add process.memoryUsage() metrics and track active CourierTracker instance count to verify the leak is fixed post-deploy.",
    "breaking_changes": {
      "api_breaking": false,
      "db_destructive": false,
      "shared_library": false,
      "auth_security": false,
      "data_pipeline": false,
      "config_changes": false,
      "details": []
    },
    "safe_to_deploy": false,
    "rollback_plan": "Revert the commit. The fix is additive (adding close handler + try/catch), so rollback simply returns to current behavior."
  },
  "escalations": [],
  "confidence": {
    "level": 95,
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Memory leak diagnosis — classic missing WebSocket close handler pattern, directly explains the reported 3-month memory growth",
      "JSON.parse crash — deterministic failure on any malformed input",
      "Input validation gap — no bounds checking on coordinates"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Whether the caller (connection manager) also retains references to CourierTracker — if so, the close handler alone is insufficient"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A new CourierTracker is created per connection as stated, implying the caller does not reuse instances",
      "db.couriers.updateLocation returns a Promise (standard for Node.js DB drivers)"
    ]
  }
}

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this code for critical issues.

Key observations:
1. Memory leak: No cleanup of WebSocket listeners or instance references when connections close
2. No JSON.parse error handling - malformed messages crash the handler
3. No input validation on lat/lng
4. No close handler - when courier disconnects, the CourierTracker instance and its listeners are never cleaned up
5. DB call has no error handling

The memory leak is the critical finding given the stated symptom.
